title: Ensure private DNS zones for private endpoints that connect  to Batch accounts are configured.

input_row.title: Ensure private DNS zones for private endpoints that connect  to Batch accounts are configured.
input_row.description: Private DNS zones for Azure Batch private endpoints provide secure internal name resolution, preventing public internet exposure. When a private endpoint is created for a Batch account, Azure requires a private DNS zone (privatelink..batch.azure.com) to map the Batch service’s domain name to a private IP address within your virtual network (VNet).
input_row.rationale: To enable secure and private access to Azure Batch accounts, private DNS zones must be properly configured for private endpoints so as not to expose them publicly and allow for internal name resolution. Proper configuration of DNS provides assurance that DNS resolves to private IPs, reducing data exposure risk and support for security policy adherence. In the absence of proper DNS configuration, Batch services are open to connectivity failure, job interruption, or misconfigured public internet routing. Well- meshed private DNS zones hold traffic within the virtual network as intended, as a Zero Trust architecture and regulatory standard would dictate. Organizations need to audit and automate the configurations for secure and stable Batch processing.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Login to Azure portal https://portal.azure.com 2. Navigate to Batch accounts in the Azure portal For each batch account perform the following: 1. Expand Settings then click on Networking 2. Click the Private access tab, If no Private endpoints exist, the configuration fails this audit procedure. For each Private endpoint perform the following: 1. Click the name of the Private Endpoint 2. Expand Settings then click on DNS Configuration 3. Scroll to the bottom where a table of Configuration entries is found Ensure that there is an entry populated in the Private DNS Zone column (e.g. privatelink.batch.azure.com) Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/batch/private-connectivity#azure-policy • Policy ID: 4ec38ebc-381f-45ee-81a4-acbc4be878f8 - Name: 'Azure Batch accounts should use private DNS zones for 'private endpoints''
input_row.remediation: NOTE: This instruction assumes a Private Endpoint already exists for the Batch Account, and that a Private DNS Zone has already been created. Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Login to Azure portal https://portal.azure.com 2. Navigate to Batch accounts in the Azure portal For each batch account perform the following: 1. Expand Settings then click on Networking 2. Click the Private access tab, [If no Private Endpoints exist, one must be created before proceeding. Instruction for configuring Private Endpoints on Batch Accounts can be found in the recommendation titled "Ensure to Configure Batch accounts with private endpoints"] For each Private endpoint perform the following: 1. Click the name of the Private Endpoint 2. Expand Settings then click on DNS Configuration 3. Click the + Add Configuration button 4. Select each field appropriately (e.g. Private DNS zone "privatelink.batch.azure.com" - other zone names in additional information below) and enter a custom Configuration Name if desired 5. Click the Add button then allow a moment for deployment Refresh the DNS Configuration page, then scroll down and ensure an entry exists (e.g. "privatelink.batch.azure.com") under the Private DNS zone column in the Configuration entry table.
input_row.impact: NOTE: This recommendation assumes that a Private DNS Zone already exists. If one has not yet been created for Batch accounts [e.g. privatelink.batch.azure.com], that must be completed before it can be assigned to a Batch account's Private Endpoint. Network architecture must be carefully considered when deploying Private DNS Zones. DNS Zones should be used to associate like services. Private DNS zones should not be used to resolve public endpoints.
input_row.references: 1. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-dns 2. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/batch/private-connectivity 3. China Zone Names: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private- endpoint-dns#compute-2
input_row.default_value: Azure DNS zones are not configured by default.
input_row.additional_information: Zone Names for resource type: Azure Batch (Microsoft.Batch/batchAccounts) Azure Commercial Cloud: 1. Subresource: batchAccount • Private DNS Zone name: privatelink.batch.azure.com • Public DNS zone forwarders: .batch.azure.com 2. Subresource: nodeManagement • Private DNS Zone name: privatelink.batch.azure.com • Public DNS zone forwarders: .service.batch.azure.com Azure Government Cloud: 1. Subresource: batchAccount • Private DNS Zone name: privatelink.batch.usgovcloudapi.net • Public DNS zone forwarders: .batch.usgovcloudapi.net 2. Subresource: nodeManagement • Private DNS Zone name: privatelink.batch.usgovcloudapi.net • Public DNS zone forwarders: .service.batch.usgovcloudapi.net Azure China Cloud: 1. Subresource: batchAccount • Private DNS Zone name: privatelink.batch.chinacloudapi.cn • Public DNS zone forwarders: .batch.chinacloudapi.cn 2. Subresource: nodeManagement • Private DNS Zone name: privatelink.batch.chinacloudapi.cn • Public DNS zone forwarders: .service.batch.chinacloudapi.cn

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Login to the Azure portal:**
   - Visit https://portal.azure.com.
   - Enter your Azure credentials to access your account.

2. **Navigate to Batch accounts:**
   - In the portal, use the left-hand menu to locate and click on “Batch accounts.”

3. **Select a Batch account:**
   - Click on the Batch account for which you want to check DNS zone configuration.

4. **Check Private Endpoints:**
   - Expand the "Settings" section and click on "Networking."
   - Navigate to the "Private access" tab.
   - Verify if any Private Endpoints exist. If no Private Endpoints are available, the configuration fails this audit procedure.

5. **Verify Private DNS zone entries for each Private Endpoint:**
   - For each Private Endpoint, click on its name to access its details.
   - Expand the "Settings" section and click on "DNS Configuration."
   - Scroll to the bottom of the DNS Configuration page to find the table of Configuration entries.
   - Ensure that there is an entry populated in the "Private DNS Zone" column (e.g., "privatelink.batch.azure.com").

6. **Document Findings:**
   - Take notes of any Batch accounts that do not have properly configured private DNS zones for their Private Endpoints.
   - Ensure documentation includes specific entries for Private DNS zone names that were missing or misconfigured.

**Expected secure configuration:**
- Every Private Endpoint should have a corresponding entry in the Private DNS Zone column populated with the correct DNS zone, e.g., "privatelink.batch.azure.com."

**How to confirm compliance:**
- If all Batch accounts have Private Endpoints configured with valid Private DNS zone entries, the control passes. If any Batch account fails to meet these criteria, it fails the compliance check.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** DISAGREE

- The original audit assessment focuses on Azure services, specifically Azure Batch and its private DNS zones. This does not directly translate to AWS services, as AWS does not have a direct equivalent to Azure Batch or its private DNS zone configuration for private endpoints. Therefore, the manual approach is not applicable to AWS.
- AWS offers services like AWS Batch, but the configuration of private DNS zones for private endpoints is handled differently, typically through Route 53 Private Hosted Zones.
- This check could be automated using AWS APIs, specifically using boto3 for AWS Route 53 to verify private hosted zones and their records. AWS CLI commands can also be used to list and verify Route 53 hosted zones and records.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 1

- The audit assessment is technically inaccurate for AWS as it references Azure-specific services and configurations.
- There are no relevant boto3 methods or AWS CLI commands provided since the assessment is not applicable to AWS.
- The manual steps are not relevant to AWS and do not follow AWS naming conventions or practices.
- The program name is not applicable as it pertains to Azure services.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- The assessment should be re-evaluated and rewritten to focus on AWS services, such as AWS Batch and Route 53.
- Consider using boto3 methods like `list_hosted_zones` and `list_resource_record_sets` to verify the existence of private hosted zones and their records in AWS.
- A better program name suggestion for AWS could be `aws_route53_private_hosted_zone_check`.

**CONFIDENCE:** LOW

- Given that the assessment is based on Azure services, my confidence in its applicability to AWS is low. The audit needs to be restructured to focus on AWS-specific services and configurations.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Re-classify: The assessment should be reclassified from a manual Azure-focused audit to an automated AWS-focused audit. This involves using AWS APIs to verify the configuration of private hosted zones in AWS Route 53 for services like AWS Batch.
- Modify: Rewrite the audit to focus on AWS services, using appropriate AWS APIs and CLI commands to automate the verification process for private DNS configurations in AWS.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The compliance control is focused on ensuring private DNS zones for private endpoints, which can be translated to AWS services using AWS Route 53 Private Hosted Zones. The second AI opinion correctly identifies that this can be automated using AWS APIs, specifically with boto3 to verify the existence and configuration of private hosted zones and their records. AWS provides the necessary API capabilities to automate this check, making an automated approach feasible and efficient.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
aws_route53_private_hosted_zone_dns_configuration_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS Resource/Service:** Route 53 Private Hosted Zones
- **API/CLI Method:** boto3 client method `list_hosted_zones()` and `list_resource_record_sets()`
- **Properties to Check:** Hosted zone name, record sets for private endpoints
- **Pass Criteria:** Private hosted zone exists with correct record sets for private endpoints
- **Fail Criteria:** Missing private hosted zone or incorrect/missing record sets

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
The first AI opinion suggested a manual approach based on Azure-specific steps, which is not applicable to AWS. The second AI correctly identified the potential for automation using AWS APIs, which aligns with AWS capabilities for managing DNS configurations through Route 53. This resolution is based on the technical feasibility of using AWS APIs to automate the compliance check.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Microsoft_Azure_Compute_Services_Benchmark_v2_0_0
